reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is an edited selective anthology, not one medieval work, one authorial collection, one witness, one edition, one encoding, or one repository file.

The edition-level boundary is twenty-nine selected parts; the intellectual count is thirty-seven. Parallel ABC, Children's Maxims, and Stans versions count once per family, while their selected versions remain explicit zero-count children.
The 3,603 non-note L count and minimally normalized 3,607-line representation are representation metrics only; aggregate abstract-work verse_lines and verse_groups remain null.

All fifty-four files under source-cache/aha6127 are modeled below with exact source URLs, checksums, byte counts, dates, coverage, and explicit target-work presence. Metadata, service, terms, inventory, and scan-structure files are supporting and marked absent even when they describe the target.
Complete IIIF ZIPs are complete provider-exposed surface inventories at the requested access profile, not claims of archival masters or every physical side beyond what the provider manifest exposes.

For inaccessible witnesses, the planned whole-object-first acquisition route begins by asking whether an existing complete surrogate can be reactivated or supplied; only then would it request a full-color whole-object reproduction with boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, endleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso. No enquiry has been submitted, and target-only images are a fallback.

The cached CME repository LICENSE contains software-worded Unlicense text. Its scope over inherited U-M transcription/XML data, medieval text, and edition content is unresolved and is not broadened beyond rights held by the affirmer; U-M's current NoC-US assertion and the embedded legacy XML availability warning are recorded separately.

Furnivall's Harley 276 heading for Fretoure is erroneous. The text is in Harley 279, f. 34v, Vyaunde Leche no. 54, confirmed through Austin's direct edition; Harley 276 is evidence of the error, never a target witness.

Current and legacy locators are not silently conflated: Urbanitatis current ff. 88r-v versus Furnivall fol. 86; Jesus current Q.G.8 versus legacy Q.T.8; and Harley 5401's printed-page/folio pairs remain dual un-equated locators.

lineage.summary: The checked-in artifact is a byte-identical copy of University of Michigan CME item AHA6127.0001.001 pinned through the CME repository at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c. The electronic item derives from Frederick J. Furnivall's 1868 edited anthology but its selected body is not one medieval work: twenty-nine edition-level parts resolve into thirty-seven intellectual items, including parallel versions, ten separately modeled recipes, three Dietary-group works, and seven Rawlinson counsel items. Furnivall's selected texts and apparatus draw on British Library, Lambeth Palace Library, Bodleian, Trinity College Cambridge, and Jesus College Cambridge witnesses; later or collateral editions by Thomas Austin and Wright and Halliwell resolve particular source questions. Complete provider-exposed public image bundles are cached for Bodleian MS Ashmole 61, Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 86, Trinity O.9.38, and Trinity R.3.19; Lambeth exposes only selected images, and no currently retrievable complete public facsimile was verified for the other principal witnesses. Access, image supply, copyright, license, and contract terms remain separate assertions.

catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-defined anthology slice represented by the pinned U-M/CME XML aha6127: twenty-nine selected edition-level parts resolving into thirty-seven intellectual items, with recipe clusters, counsel clusters, and parallel witness versions modeled explicitly. It is not one medieval work, one authorial collection, one manuscript, every item in Furnivall's 1868 volume, the print edition itself, the electronic encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Medieval authorship, date, place, language, form, and genre are asserted only at part level where the evidence permits.

contentParts.label: The Babees Book
contentParts.summary: A courtesy poem instructing young people of high status in virtuous behavior and table conduct.

contentParts.label: The ABC of Aristotle version family

contentParts.label: Lerne or be Lewde (Harley version)

contentParts.label: Symon's Lesson of Wisdom for All Manner Children

scope.description: Furnivall's publication-layer selection and arrangement, not one medieval composition event

notes: No blanket composition date is assigned to the thirty-seven heterogeneous medieval items.

scope.description: The U-M electronic item represented by the pinned XML

extent.description: Thirty-seven countable intellectual items are represented by forty-seven content-part records: twenty-one top-level manifest parts abstracting twenty-three selected top-level XML divisions, six zero-count parallel-version children, ten recipe children, three Dietary-group children, and seven Rawlinson-counsel children. The XML contains 3,603 non-note L elements. A minimal normalized selected representation is 3,607 metrical lines after resolving the Reward's two encoded prose-like L elements as four metrical lines and the Dietary introductory P as a two-line couplet. Neither 3,603 nor 3,607 is an aggregate abstract-work extent because the XML prints parallel versions of three families; aggregate verse_lines and verse_groups are therefore null.

formStatements.description: The anthology contains twenty-five verse-only and twelve prose-only intellectual items. Parallel versions count once at family level; recipe and counsel containers are not additional intellectual items.

scope.description: Furnivall's 1868 selection, transcription, arrangement, titles, notes, and apparatus, not medieval authorship
The heterogeneous edited anthology and its ordering

notes: The separate introductory couplet and embedded Precepts in -ly are not absorbed into Lydgate's authorship responsibility.
This preserves an ascribed/traditional authorship rather than converting the title attribution into certainty.
Possible adaptation or revision and the sixteen-line Harleian epilogue's copy-supervision context remain distinct from the principal attribution.
Only the four-line extract corresponding to Instructions lines 64–67 is assigned to Idley.
Grosseteste is an associated household authority and traditional title figure; Adam de Marisco is a conjectural Latin-source author. Neither is made the secure author of this translation.
The transmitted name is preserved without identifying the person or converting a signature into secure authorship.

summaries.value: The selected anthology combines courtesy and conduct poems, parental instruction, dietary advice, ten culinary recipes, household statutes, short counsel texts, and several miscellaneous didactic pieces. Furnivall constructs the reading text from many manuscripts and, in three families, prints parallel witness versions. The exact selected body is therefore modeled as thirty-seven intellectual items nested under twenty-nine edition-level parts rather than as one medieval work.

The XML body has twenty-three selected non-omitted top-level divisions. Splitting each five-recipe cluster adds eight edition parts, while collapsing the two ABC divisions and the two Children's Maxims divisions into two version families subtracts two, yielding twenty-nine edition-level parts. Four zero-count containers then decompose the Dietary group and Rawlinson counsel cluster, producing thirty-seven countable intellectual items. Version children are represented but do not add intellectual items.

notes: The qualifier exposes the heterogeneous electronic selection boundary and prevents the anthology from being mistaken for one medieval work.
